title: Behind the curve: Britain’s defence investment plan in Trump’s world – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Nick Witney argues Britain's newly published Defence Investment Plan (DIP) under-matches the Russian threat, fails to internalise Ukraine's lessons on next-generation warfare, and remains dangerously tethered to a US relationship Trump has already upended — even as British public opinion has moved further and faster toward Europe than the defence establishment has.

insights.claim: Britain's Defence Investment Plan concludes that credible Integrated Air and Missile Defence 'can only be accomplished as part of a NATO endeavour,' effectively outsourcing UK homeland air defence rather than building sovereign capacity.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Signals Britain will remain a price-taker on air-defence procurement (missiles, radars) rather than a driver of sovereign or MBDA-led European capacity — relevant for European defence-industrial allocation.
insights.quote: Capable and effective Integrated Air and Missile Defence can only be accomplished as part of a NATO endeavour
insights.locator: However, the plan reaches the extraordinary conclusion

insights.claim: A single misdirected drone brought down the Latvian government in May 2026, illustrating how low the threshold is for a hybrid incident to cause political collapse in a NATO state with inadequate air defence.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent precedent for how cheap drone incursions can generate outsized political shocks — a risk factor for European political-stability and defence-spending forecasts.
insights.locator: It took only one misdirected drone

insights.claim: The DIP has no proposals for an emergency ramp-up of air-defence missile production by MBDA, Europe's leading missile maker (one-third British-owned), despite a proliferation of national 'air shield' projects across Europe that Britain is not coordinating with.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Flags an unfunded, uncoordinated gap in European air-defence missile capacity — a potential order-flow catalyst for MBDA if UK policy shifts, and a coordination risk if it doesn't.
insights.locator: Where are the proposals for an emergency ramp-up

insights.claim: Ukraine's battlefield success demonstrates a new warfare paradigm — a web of sensors and AI-assisted battle-management systems targeting via missiles, drones and cyber/electronic countermeasures, with development and procurement cycles compressed to weeks or days and hardware/software updates within hours — replacing the cold-war currency of aircraft, ships and tanks.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Defines the capability shift investors should track: procurement-cycle speed and software/AI integration now matter more than legacy platform counts for defence-tech winners.
insights.locator: Those lessons are in effect the development of a new paradigm

insights.claim: Despite rhetorical embrace of drones and a new Digital Targeting Web, the bulk of DIP investment still flows to next-generation legacy platforms — the Tempest sixth-generation fighter, the nuclear deterrent, the AUKUS nuclear-powered attack submarine programme, and helicopters — the capabilities the traditional defence industry and regional-jobs politics favour.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Identifies where UK defence capital is actually flowing (Tempest/AUKUS/nuclear primes) versus where doctrine says it should — a signal for which legacy defence contractors keep order backlogs.
insights.locator: But the balance of overall investment

insights.claim: The DIP commits to 'ruthlessly prioritise and make tough short-term choices, disinvesting in [old] capabilities,' yet in practice this amounts only to retiring some old-style destroyers and frigates, while mothballing the increasingly irrelevant aircraft carriers was rejected — producing a 'hybridisation' of new capability grafted onto old.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Suggests UK defence reform is more rhetorical than structural, limiting fiscal headroom for the sensor/drone/AI capabilities the article argues actually matter.
insights.quote: ruthlessly prioritise and make tough short-term choices, disinvesting in [old] capabilities

insights.claim: The DIP's new drone investment (£5bn) is weighted toward underwater robotics and cyber/electromagnetic defence, framed as a response to Putin's likely pivot to sabotage, subversion, cyber assaults and infrastructure attacks (undersea cables/pipes) rather than conventional invasion, should Russia's position in Ukraine keep deteriorating.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Flags undersea infrastructure protection and underwater robotics as a growth area within UK defence procurement even as broader air-defence spending lags.
insights.locator: In this regard, the DIP rightly focuses

insights.claim: The DIP endorses buying more American F-35 aircraft to carry US free-fall nuclear bombs even though half a dozen European NATO allies already provide this capability, meaning the additional deterrent value is minimal while sovereignty risk is real: the aircraft's source code is withheld by the US and rumoured to contain a US-operated 'kill switch.'
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: A specific, quantifiable sovereignty risk (software kill-switch exposure) in a major platform purchase — relevant to assessing European 'strategic autonomy' procurement shifts away from US primes.

insights.claim: Witney draws a direct analogy between the F-35's alleged remote kill-switch risk and real precedents of US-controlled software disabling hardware remotely: John Deere remotely killed tractors stolen from Ukraine by Russian forces, and Tesla cars carry a similar remote-disable feature.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: A concrete precedent-based argument for why buyers of US-software-embedded hardware (defence or civilian) face real remote-disablement risk — relevant to procurement diversification theses.
insights.locator: Compare the fate of American tractors

insights.claim: Continental Europeans are increasingly treating excessive technological dependence on America as a serious risk: calls for 'strategic autonomy' and 'buy European,' once dismissed as Gaullist eccentricity, are now mainstream, and efforts to build national alternatives to US big-tech AI-driven command systems are proliferating.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: A structural tailwind for European defence-tech and AI-command-system vendors displacing US incumbents — a theme to track across EU defence procurement.
insights.locator: Continental Europeans are now taking the issue

insights.claim: Palantir is now well established within the UK's defence-transformation efforts; Witney argues incoming PM Andy Burnham should address European mistrust of Britain's cross-Channel commitment by ensuring the contract for the new Digital Targeting Web does not go to US big tech.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Names a live, contestable procurement decision (Digital Targeting Web) where the outcome will signal whether the UK tilts toward US big tech (Palantir) or European alternatives.

insights.claim: A year after the EU-UK summit, Britain's anticipated fast-track admission to the EU's main defence-industrial collaboration programme has still not materialised, showing that Britain's 'defence card' commands less leverage with Europe than London hoped.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Evidence that UK defence contributions alone are not securing preferential EU market access — relevant to assessing pace of UK defence-industrial integration with the EU.
insights.locator: a year on from last year’s EU-UK summit

themes.theme: Public opinion ahead of the establishment

insights.claim: Recent ECFR polling shows British public opinion holds both EU member states and the EU itself in much greater esteem than the US, with far more Britons believing continental neighbours would help repel an attack than trusting America to do so; 63% would prioritise defence relations with Europe versus 19% for the US.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: A concrete polling data point (63% vs 19%) showing UK public opinion is well ahead of establishment defence policy in tilting toward Europe — a leading indicator for future UK-EU defence-industrial policy shifts under Burnham.
insights.locator: So the results thrown up by recent ECFR polling

insights.claim: Incoming PM Andy Burnham may find he has more political room for manoeuvre than expected on defence investment given that public opinion already favours a Europe-first posture over reliance on the US.
insights.speaker: Nick Witney
insights.so_what: Suggests policy space exists for a more Europe-oriented UK defence-industrial pivot once Burnham takes office, which would be a catalyst for European (vs US) defence suppliers.
insights.locator: When he takes office later this month
